works.medium: Silkscreen print with embossing on cotton rag paper
works.title: Positive Space/Negative Space (Cream/Red)
works.description: Part of the Obey Invader series: a collaboration between Shepard Fairey and Invader produced for the Triple Trouble exhibition at Newport Street Gallery, London. One of four prints in the series, this edition brings together Fairey's bold graphic language and Invader's pixelated tile aesthetic, with embossing adding a tactile dimension to the silkscreen surface.
works.edition: Edition of 250, hand signed by both artists on the front. Presented in a white aluminium frame

artists.name: Invader
artists.bio: Invader (b. 1969, Paris) is an anonymous French street artist and graduate of a Parisian École des Beaux-Arts, who describes himself as an Unidentified Free Artist (UFA) — his identity perpetually hidden behind masks and digital pixelations. Beginning in the late 1990s, he started cementing ceramic tile mosaics of pixelated characters from the 1978 Space Invaders arcade game across the streets of Paris, before expanding his roster to include Pac-Man ghosts and other 8-bit characters, works that now adorn cities around the world, from Los Angeles to Kathmandu. As of 2020, his creations can be found in 79 cities across 20 countries, comprising over 3,800 individual works and more than 1.5 million ceramic tiles. Beyond public space, he has developed "Rubikcubism" (a series of indoor works constructed entirely from Rubik's Cubes) and his gallery works regularly command six-figure sums.

artists.name: Shepard Fairey
artists.bio: Shepard Fairey (b. 1970, Charleston, South Carolina) is an American graphic artist and social activist at the forefront of the street art movement, whose work blurs the boundary between traditional and commercial art through type and image, communicating social critique via prints, murals, stickers, and posters in public spaces. He graduated from the Rhode Island School of Design in 1992, and in 1989 launched the André the Giant Has a Posse sticker campaign — the foundation for his seminal Obey series. He is perhaps best known for the Obama Hope poster of 2008, widely considered the most successful presidential campaign poster ever created, and his works are held in the permanent collections of MoMA, the Smithsonian's National Portrait Gallery, the Victoria and Albert Museum, and LACMA.

artists.name: Paul Kremer
artists.bio: Paul Kremer (b. 1971, Chicago) is a contemporary American artist known for his reinterpretation of colour field painting, using overlapping hard-edged shapes to produce optical interactions rooted as much in digital software as in the work of Kenneth Noland and Jules Olitski. Self-taught as a painter, his uninflected surfaces and smooth contours have led critics to identify him as an heir to Ellsworth Kelly, with his compositions evoking both monumental architecture and broken-off glacier sections. For twenty years Kremer ran a graphic design studio, working with clients including Lou Reed, Tom Waits, and National Geographic, and he is also the inventor of the cult social media phenomenon "Great Art in Ugly Rooms," which places photo-realistic renderings of masterworks in laundromats, discount stores, and public restrooms.

artists.name: Yoon Hyup
artists.bio: Born in Seoul, South Korea in 1982, Yoon Hyup lives and works in Brooklyn, New York. Drawn to hip-hop and skate culture from an early age, he began creating large-scale murals and live paintings in the early 2000s, experimenting with lines and dots as a way of reflecting the rhythm and flow of music. Working with acrylic on canvas, he translates cityscapes, rain, and light into compositions of flat orbs and rhythmic marks, comparing his process to jazz improvisation, where each colour represents a different note and together they build a chord. Hyup has exhibited in New York, London, Seoul, Milan, Tokyo, Miami, and Hong Kong, and has collaborated with Dior, Tiffany & Co., Nike, and Maison Kitsuné, among others

artists.name: The Miaz Brothers
artists.bio: Roberto and Renato Miaz (b. 1965 & 1968, Monza, Italy) are an Italian sibling duo who have redefined contemporary portraiture through their "Antimatter" series; large-format works rendered in aerosol paint, in which subjects are presented deliberately out of focus, bypassing immediate reaction and inviting viewers to draw on their own memory and perception to complete the image. Classically trained and guided by the model of Renaissance artists, they first exhibited at the Viafarini Gallery in Milan in 1996, before being awarded the Arte Laguna International Art Prize in Venice in 2011 and launching the Antimatter series in 2012. They have collaborated with major brands including Adidas, Nike, Diesel, and Kenzo, and designed the cover of the New York Times Magazine in 2017.

artists.name: Ben Eine
artists.bio: Ben Eine (born Ben Flynn, b. 1970, London) is a British street artist who began his career as a graffiti writer, tagging the streets of his home city before developing the distinctive typographic style he is known for today. He later co-founded the cult screen print company Pictures on Walls (POW) with Banksy, producing hand-pulled prints for artists including Jamie Hewlett, David Shrigley, and Mode 2. He is regarded as a forerunner in the exploration of typography in contemporary street art, best known for his oversized, brightly coloured letterforms on shop shutters - most famously the full alphabet he painted onto Middlesex Street in East London, known internationally as Alphabet Street. In 2010, British Prime Minister David Cameron gifted one of Eine's paintings, Twenty First Century City, to President Barack Obama during a state visit — catapulting Eine to international fame and selling out his subsequent San Francisco solo show before it opened. His work is held in the collection of the Victoria and Albert Museum.

artists.name: David Shrigley
artists.bio: David Shrigley OBE (b. 1968, Macclesfield) is a British artist best known for his distinctive drawing style and satirical commentary on everyday situations and human interactions. His quick-witted drawings and hand-rendered texts are typically deadpan in their humour, capturing chance utterings, overheard conversations, and the compulsive habits of everyday life. While drawing is at the centre of his practice, Shrigley also works across sculpture, large-scale installation, animation, painting, photography, and music; most notably his monumental sculpture Really Good, unveiled on the Fourth Plinth in Trafalgar Square in 2016. His works are held in the collections of MoMA New York, the Art Institute of Chicago, the Centre Pompidou, the Tate, and the Thyssen-Bornemisza Contemporary Art Foundation, and he was shortlisted for the Turner Prize in 2013.

artists.name: Oli Epp
artists.bio: Oli Epp (b. 1994, London) is a contemporary painter whose tragicomic works explore identity, consumerism, addiction, and anxiety in the digital age. Working with a combination of airbrush, oil, and acrylic to create his flat, graphic style (which he describes as "Post-Digital Pop") Epp depicts contorted, faceless figures surrounded by the slick pleasures of contemporary material culture: money, cigarettes, logos, and fast food. He received his BA in Fine Art from the City & Guilds of London Art School and has exhibited in Paris, New York, London, Berlin, and Los Angeles, with institutional shows at the Schlossmuseum Linz and the Museum of Fine Arts Leipzig. His work is held in the collections of the V&A, the Museo Nacional Thyssen-Bornemisza, and the Museum of Modern Art, Paris.

Sweet Toof is an anonymous British graffiti and street artist who began tagging at age 13, saving lunch money to buy cheap spray cans, before going on to graduate with a master's degree from the Royal Academy of Arts. A member of the Burning Candy Crew, he began painting the streets of East London, where his signature motif, a set of grinning pearly white teeth behind bright pink gums, became one of the most recognisable images in the city. Equally disciplined in traditional painting and printmaking, Sweet Toof masterfully blends urban detritus with bygone decadence, fusing ancient methods with modern materials to create work that is at once iconoclastic and deeply rooted in art history. His work featured in Banksy's documentary Exit Through the Gift Shop and is held in the permanent collection of the Victoria and Albert Museum.
artists.name: Sweet Toof
Jason Revok
artists.bio: Jason Revok (b. 1977, Riverside, California) is a self-taught artist whose practice began in the Los Angeles graffiti scene of the 1990s, where he built a reputation as one of the most prolific writers of his generation before pivoting to an ambitious studio practice driven by minimalism and post-painterly abstraction. Refusing to be limited by his early recognition, Revok allows only certain elements from graffiti culture to transition to his contemporary work (modest materials, industrial tools, ingenuity) while his proclivity towards systematic yet imperfect mark-making has become the driving force behind his practice. In 2011 he relocated to Detroit, where he founded the Detroit Beautification Project, and in 2022 his solo exhibition was mounted at the Museum of Contemporary Art Detroit (MOCAD).

Gary Stranger is a largely self-taught British artist who took up graffiti in 1996 and went on to become a member of the MSK street art crew, before merging the aesthetics of street art with his deep interest in typography in the late 2000s. Recognised for their minimal aesthetic, precise freehand execution, and bold colour choices, his works typically depict a single word or phrase in a three-dimensional typographic style, produced across murals, studio paintings, works on paper, and sculpture. He is co-founder, alongside Pref, of Typograffic Circle; a collective of artists working at the intersection of graffiti and typographic design, taking placement and individual style from the street and combining it with the constraints of type-making. His work was included in the Royal Academy of Arts' 2020 Winter Exhibition, and in 2025 he was featured in Graffitecture: Typographic Blueprints at STRAAT Museum, Amsterdam.
artists.name: Gary Stranger
DRAN
artists.bio: Dran (b. 1979, Toulouse) is a French artist frequently referred to as "the French Banksy" for his satirical and sharp critique of contemporary society. As a child he copied images from comics; by 13 he was painting on walls, before going on to study at the École des Beaux-Arts in Toulouse. His work unpacks themes of creativity and freedom of expression through surrealistic characters (many of them children carrying crayons) alongside popes, riot police, clowns, and pop culture icons such as Pinocchio, Tintin, and the Mona Lisa. A member of the Da Mental Vaporz crew (DMV) since 1999, Dran works across graffiti, illustration, stencil, painting, silkscreen, and photography. He chooses to stay out of the media spotlight entirely, letting the work speak for itself. A quality that has only deepened his cult following among collectors.

Damien Hirst (b. 1965, Bristol) has created installations, sculptures, paintings, and drawings since the late 1980s that examine the complex relationships between art and beauty, religion and science, and life and death. Considered an enfant terrible of the 1990s art world, he was the most prominent of the Young British Artists - a group that first gained notoriety when Charles Saatchi began buying and exhibiting their work, and in 1988 curated the landmark student show Freeze at Goldsmiths College, widely considered the debut of the YBA movement. His mixed-media sculptures draw from a controversial assortment of formaldehyde-suspended carcasses, cigarette butts, pharmaceutical packaging, and surgical instruments, often encased in glass vitrines. He won the Turner Prize in 1995, and in 2008 set the record for the largest sum raised by a living artist at a single auction, with £111 million at Sotheby's. In 2015 he opened Newport Street Gallery in London, and his work is held by Tate, the Hirshhorn, and major collections worldwide.
artists.name: Damien Hirst

artists.bio: Marina Abramović (b. 1946, Belgrade) has pioneered the use of performance as a visual art form since the early 1970s, making her body both subject and medium - exploring physical and mental limits, endurance, pain, and the possibility of spiritual transformation. She studied at the Academy of Fine Arts in Belgrade and completed postgraduate studies in Zagreb, before conceiving a series of visceral performance pieces that used her body as a site of artistic and spiritual exploration. From 1975 to 1988 she collaborated with the German artist Ulay on works exploring duality and gender identity, ending their partnership in a symbolic performance in which each walked the Great Wall of China from opposite ends to meet in the middle. In 2023 she became the first female artist to host a major solo exhibition in the Main Galleries of the Royal Academy of Arts in London. She has referred to herself as "the grandmother of performance art",
artists.name: Marina Abramović
Faile
artists.bio: FAILE is the Brooklyn-based multimedia collaboration of Patrick McNeil (b. 1975, Edmonton) and Patrick Miller (b. 1976, Minneapolis), formed in 1999. Their name is an anagram of their original pseudonym "A Life," reflecting a philosophy of transformation and the belief that failure is a necessary step toward more enlightened art and life. Their practice involves a frenetic collaging of appropriated materials (graphic novels, Asian and American culture, typography) that references the work of Pop artists like Richard Hamilton and Roy Lichtenstein, deployed initially through pioneering wheatpasting and stenciling on city streets worldwide before expanding into paintings, prints, and sculpture. An important show at Tate Modern in 2008 marked their transition into the institutional art world, and in 2015 they were commissioned for a major installation in Times Square, New York.

artists.name: Hush
artists.bio: Hush (b. 1973, Newcastle upon Tyne) is a British artist who developed an early affinity for visual culture amid the Acid House scene, designing flyers before pursuing formal training at the Newcastle School of Art and Design, graduating in 1995 with a degree in graphic design and illustration. His career pivoted decisively around 2006 when he committed fully to painting, departing from graphic design roles at advertising agencies in London and Hong Kong. Extensive time living in Asia (first Hong Kong, then Japan, where he worked as a toy designer) immersed him in Japanese visual culture and sparked a fascination with the representation of women and the collision of Eastern and Western aesthetics. In his signature works, Hush superimposes layers of vibrant wheatpaste patterns and graffiti onto delicate stencilled portraits of women, fusing the iconography of the geisha with the raw energy of street art. He lives and works in Newcastle and has been named one of the top 20 emerging artists by The Independent.

journal.title: Understanding the edition market in 2024
journal.author: UNVLD. Editorial
journal.excerpt: The edition market has undergone a significant recalibration. Here's what serious collectors need to know.
journal.body:
The edition market has always operated on its own logic — distinct from the primary market for originals, and often misunderstood by collectors approaching it for the first time.

In 2024, that logic has shifted. Supply has expanded significantly, buyer behaviour has changed, and the gap between works that hold value and those that do not has widened considerably.

What's changed

The past three years have seen a significant increase in edition releases across all categories. Artists who previously released once or twice a year are now releasing quarterly.

What it means for collectors

Selectivity matters more than ever. The works that will hold their ground are those with genuine cultural weight — artists with sustained institutional presence, editions with low print runs and strong secondary market histories.
